text: Hearken to the reed-flute, how it discourses When complaining of the pains of separation - " Ever since they tore me from my osier bed, My plaintive notes have moved men and women to tears. I burst my breast, striving to give vent to sighs, And to express the pangs of my yearning for my home.
author: Jalal al-Din Rumi
locator: Book I, proem (the Song of the Reed)
source: archive.org full text: 'Masnavi i Ma'navi; the spiritual couplets', trans. E. H. Whinfield (item masnaviimanavi)
out_pulls_note: High heat: the reed torn from its bed, wailing to return home, is pure ache -- a stranger feels the longing as their own before any doctrine of self-dissolution is mentioned.
search_notes: E. H. Whinfield abridged translation, 1898 (archive.org item 'masnaviimanavi', title page credits 'E. H. WHINFIELD, M.A.'); chosen as confirmed PD. Verbatim substring confirmed after normalising whitespace (the OCR uses double spaces between words), em-dash->hyphen, and curly->straight quotes. NOTE: the source OCR places a space after the opening quotation mark ('separation - " Ever since'), so the verbatim span carries that extra space; kept honest rather than tidied. The famous reed-flute proem, the canonical jolt for fana. UNSETTLED concept: avoided any claim that the passage proves/settles mystical union; it is one tradition's image of it, not a verdict. Confidence high on the text; rights confirmed (Whinfield 1898, PD).
